title: Streetline-style real-estate interior edit: straighten, HDR-look tone, white balance, window pull, outlet clean-up
grounding_note: VERBATIM from Kimberley's Upwork brief (db_i 3084): Dutch real-estate photographer needs an editing partner for a 25-30 photo batch; named services = "HDR merge / exposure blending", "Vertical and horizontal line correction", "Color correction and white balance adjustment", "Natural window views (window pull)", "Removal of distracting objects where necessary", "General image enhancement while maintaining a realistic appearance"; quality bar = "Natural-looking colors", "Bright but realistic interiors", "Straight walls and vertical lines", "Preserving outdoor views through windows", "No oversaturated colors or unrealistic HDR effects", style "similar to Streetline: bright, clean, realistic, and professional"; delivery = "High-resolution JPG files" with "Consistent editing style across all images". REASONABLY ENRICHED for a runnable single-image benchmark: the brief is a 25-30 image batch, so I instantiate the named edit recipe on ONE representative hero living-room frame plus the client's bracketed over-exposed reference (which carries the blown-out window) — the same chain is the per-image recipe that would be looped across the set. HONEST CAPABILITY MAPPING required by the sheet: (a) true multi-bracket HDR MERGE is not a single connector tool, so the "HDR / exposure blending" requirement is delivered as the industry-equivalent single-frame tonal recovery (apply_auto_tone + selective highlight/shadow/exposure recovery) that produces the bright-but-realistic Streetline look without an unrealistic HDR effect — exactly what the client asks for ("no unrealistic HDR effects"); (b) "window pull" is implemented legally as select_by_prompt(windows) -> highlight/exposure pulldown on that mask (selective tonal edit, explicitly allowed), NOT a prompt-based composite; (c) "removal of distracting objects" via AI is [X] not available, so I scope it to the connector-legal case the brief allows ("where necessary"): a single small fixed-color distractor (a wall power outlet) is selected and solid-filled to match the wall via image_fill_area — no generative fill, no AI object removal. No Canva, no Express [W] widget track, no generative fill, no compositing.
one_line_ask: Edit a Dutch real-estate interior to a bright, clean, realistic Streetline-style finish — straighten verticals, recover an HDR-look exposure, fix white balance, pull the blown-out window view back, neutralize one distracting wall outlet, and deliver a high-res JPG.
full_brief:
Kimberley is launching a real-estate photography business in the Netherlands and needs a reliable editing partner for an initial batch of 25-30 listing photos shot for the Dutch/European market (Streetline-style expectations). Using ONE representative hero frame of a living room (and the client's matching over-exposed bracket that still holds the exterior window detail), run the standard per-image edit recipe end-to-end on the Adobe connector so it can be looped across the whole set with a consistent style:

1) Load the client's two supplied frames into CC storage (the well-exposed hero interior + the brighter bracket reference that preserves the window-view exterior).
2) Auto-straighten the hero to fix tilted verticals/horizontals (walls must read straight), then crop to clean bounds removing the rotation wedge.
3) Apply auto-tone, then recover exposure / lift the dark portions / tame the highlights to build the bright-but-realistic, HDR-style interior WITHOUT an oversaturated or unrealistic HDR look.
4) Neutralize the warm/cool color cast: correct color temperature for accurate white balance, then gently lift vibrance (no oversaturation) so colors stay natural.
5) Window pull: select the windows by prompt, pull their highlights and exposure down on that mask so the outdoor view (garden/street) reads through the glass instead of blowing out white.
6) Clean one distracting element where necessary: select the small white wall power outlet and solid-fill it to match the surrounding wall color (a legal solid-fill clean-up, not AI object removal).
7) Apply a final consistent, realistic look and finalize the upload as the delivered high-resolution JPG, plus an inline preview for client approval.

Deliverable: one high-resolution, Streetline-ready JPG of the interior — straight verticals, neutral white balance, bright realistic interior, intact through-window exterior view, distractor cleaned — that defines the repeatable editing style for the remaining images in the batch.

inputs.name: hero_livingroom_interior.jpg
inputs.gen_prompt: Photoreal real-estate listing photo of an empty modern Dutch living room shot on a wide-angle lens at chest height, taken slightly tilted so the vertical wall lines lean a few degrees (un-straightened, as a raw real-estate capture). Bright daylight interior but the tall garden-facing windows on the left are slightly blown out to near-white. Light oak engineered floor, white plaster walls, a grey three-seat sofa, a low wooden TV console, a green potted plant. One small white double power outlet visible low on the right-hand wall as a distracting element. Realistic mixed daylight/tungsten color cast (slightly warm), natural shadows, real-world imperfections, fine sensor noise, documentary architectural-photography feel, NOT a glossy CGI render. 3:2, high resolution.
inputs.realism_notes: Must look like an unedited raw real-estate capture: intentionally tilted verticals, a real (not stylized) slight warm white-balance cast, and genuinely near-blown windows so the straighten/white-balance/window-pull steps have something real to correct. Visible small wall outlet so the fill_area clean-up has a real fixed-color target. Real floor grain and sensor noise, no CGI gloss.

inputs.name: window_bracket_reference.jpg
inputs.gen_prompt: The SAME Dutch living-room scene and identical camera position as the hero frame, but exposed about two stops darker (a bracket exposure) so the tall left-hand garden windows are correctly exposed and the outdoor view — a green Dutch back garden with a wooden fence and grey sky — is clearly visible through the glass, while the room interior is now dim and underexposed. Same lens, same tilt, photoreal, documentary real-estate feel, fine sensor noise, no CGI gloss. 3:2, high resolution.
inputs.realism_notes: Pixel-aligned darker bracket of the identical scene so it is a believable client-supplied exposure-blend reference; the only reason to keep it is the correctly-exposed exterior window view, supporting the honest framing of the HDR-blend/window-pull requirement.

outputs.name: livingroom_streetline_final.jpg
outputs.spec: High-resolution JPG (3:2, full sensor resolution preserved, sRGB), Streetline-style finish: straightened verticals, cropped clean (no rotation wedge), bright-but-realistic interior with recovered highlights/shadows, neutral white balance, natural non-oversaturated color, windows pulled so the exterior garden view reads through the glass, single wall outlet neutralized to wall color, consistent realistic final look. Delivered as the client-approved master that sets the editing style for the remaining 24-29 images.

outputs.name: livingroom_streetline_final_preview
outputs.spec: Inline web preview (bounded JPEG/PNG render) of the finalized image for in-chat client sign-off before batch roll-out.

connector_workflow.note: Begin upload of the client's hero living-room frame into CC storage; returns upload session + target href.
Finalize the hero upload to a usable CC asset URN/presigned URL — the base image the whole edit chain consumes.
Begin upload of the darker window-bracket reference (carries the correctly-exposed exterior) so it lives alongside the hero for the window-pull target reference.
Finalize the bracket reference asset; used as the visual ground-truth for how the pulled window exterior should read.
Auto-straighten the tilted hero so wall verticals/horizontals read straight — the brief's 'Vertical and horizontal line correction'.
Crop away the rotation wedge introduced by straightening for a clean rectangular frame.
Baseline auto-tone to set sane exposure/contrast before targeted HDR-look recovery.
Lift the dark/shadow portions to open up the interior — the 'bright but realistic' Streetline look, exposure-blend equivalent on a single frame.
Pull global highlights down to recover blown areas without an unrealistic HDR halo (brief: 'no unrealistic HDR effects').
Correct the warm/cool cast for accurate white balance — the brief's 'Color correction and white balance adjustment'.
Gently lift vibrance only (no saturation push) so colors stay natural — brief: 'Natural-looking colors', 'No oversaturated colors'.
Select the windows by prompt to build the mask for the window pull (target view validated against bracket_asset).
On the window mask, pull highlights down so the blown glass recovers toward the exterior garden view — selective masked tonal edit.
Further reduce exposure on the same window mask to fully resolve the through-glass garden view — completes 'Natural window views (window pull)'.
Select the small white wall power outlet — the single distracting element to clean 'where necessary'.
Solid-fill the outlet selection with the wall's neutral color (legal solid-color fill, NOT AI object removal) to neutralize the distractor.
Apply one consistent realistic interior look (a neutral bright-clean preset, not a stylized filter) to lock the repeatable per-image style across the batch.
Finalize the edited result into CC storage as the delivered high-resolution Streetline-ready master JPG.
Render an inline preview of the final master for in-chat client sign-off before looping the recipe across the remaining images.

chaining_note: Strict single-thread output->input chain. Inputs load first: hero_livingroom_interior.jpg -> (1 init -> 2 finalize) hero_asset; window_bracket_reference.jpg -> (3 init -> 4 finalize) bracket_asset. The edit chain then flows hero_asset -> 5 auto_straighten -> straightened_img -> 6 crop_to_bounds -> cropped_img -> 7 auto_tone -> autotoned_img -> 8 dark_portions -> shadows_lifted_img -> 9 highlights -> highlights_tamed_img -> 10 color_temperature -> wb_corrected_img -> 11 vibrance_and_saturation -> color_balanced_img. The window pull forks a mask off color_balanced_img: 12 select_by_prompt -> window_mask, which together with color_balanced_img AND bracket_asset (the correctly-exposed reference frame) feeds 13 highlights(masked) -> window_highlights_pulled_img -> 14 exposure(masked, same window_mask) -> window_pulled_img. Thus bracket_asset (loaded at steps 3-4) is an actively consumed inputs_from reference at step 13, not merely a described one. The outlet clean-up forks again: 15 select_by_prompt(window_pulled_img) -> outlet_mask, then 16 fill_area(window_pulled_img + outlet_mask) -> outlet_cleaned_img -> 17 apply_preset -> final_look_img -> 18 get_presigned_urls -> livingroom_streetline_final.jpg -> 19 inline_preview -> preview. Every step after the initial asset loads consumes a prior step's named output; the deliverable JPG is the terminal product of the chain.
difficulty_rationale: Long-horizon and wide-mix: 19 connector calls, all server="adobe", 14 distinct Adobe tools spanning three groups (asset upload/preview, geometry, tonal/color, masked selection, solid-fill clean-up, preset). Difficulty comes from honest capability mapping under hard constraints: the brief's tempting-but-illegal asks (true HDR merge, AI distractor removal, prompt-based window replacement) are each re-expressed as connector-legal equivalents — single-frame tonal recovery for the HDR look, masked highlight/exposure pulldown for the window pull, and solid-fill for the one distractor — with no generative fill, no compositing, no upscaling, and no Express/Canva. The masked two-stage window pull (select -> highlights -> exposure on the same mask) and the select->fill outlet clean-up require correct mask reuse and order. All steps are [C] headless-confirmed, so the whole chain is autonomously runnable while still reading as a real Streetline-grade per-image editing recipe.

reverify.banned_capability_check: No banned/[X] capability is invoked anywhere in the chain. HDR is delivered as single-frame tonal recovery (auto_tone + dark_portions/highlights/exposure), the window pull is a masked selective tonal edit (select_by_prompt -> masked image_adjust_highlights/image_adjust_exposure), and the one distractor is a SOLID-COLOR image_fill_area of a selected region (not AI object removal, not generative fill, not compositing). No image_crop_and_resize/reframe-crop, no upscale/super-resolution, no text-to-image, no from-scratch art/logo, no bg-replace-by-prompt, no Canva/Express widget track. image_fill_area is used only as a solid fill within the existing canvas (no canvas growth). Every step is a real Adobe connector tool in exec_mode C (headless). Step 18 now uses asset_get_presigned_urls (real connector tool) to surface the edit-chain output for delivery instead of the imprecise asset_finalize_file_upload; bracket_asset is now an actively consumed inputs_from reference at step 13. All-real-connector, all gen-free.
reverify.notes: Both flagged issues resolved with minimal edits. (1) Step 18 asset_finalize_file_upload -> asset_get_presigned_urls (matches findings recommendation; retrieves a presigned URL for the edit output rather than finalizing a non-existent upload session). (2) bracket_asset dead-weight resolved by adding it to step 13 inputs_from as the correctly-exposed reference frame guiding the window-highlight pull, so it is now an actual consumed input, not only a described reference. tool_call_count unchanged at 19; distinct_adobe_tools 14 -> 15 (asset_get_presigned_urls added; asset_finalize_file_upload still used at steps 2 and 4). Task remains real, coherent, and fully connector-doable.
